titleStmt.title: N. 353. Prayer of the mahānāvika Buddhagupta (ca. 7th c. CE)

licence.p: This work is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 Unported Licence. To view a copy of the licence, visit https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ or send a letter to Creative Commons, 444 Castro Street, Suite 900, Mountain View, California, 94041, USA.

projectDesc.p: The project DHARMA has received funding from the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme (grant agreement no 809994).

prefixDef.p: Public URIs with the prefix bib to point to a Zotero Group Library named ERC-DHARMA whose data are open to the public.
Internal URIs using the part prefix to point to person elements in the DHARMA_idListMembers_v01.xml file.

div.p:
Top
śrī-sarvvajña-pādau śirasā praṇipatya Left
sarvveṇa prakāreṇa sarvvasmin sarvvathā-sarvva
-siddhayātrāḥ santu Right
sav r
mahānāvika-buddhaguptasya raktamr̥ir̥ttika-vāstavyasya

p.q: Through ignorance, karma is accumulated; karma is the cause of rebirth; through knowledge, karma is not accumulated; in the absence of karma, one is not reborn.

div.p: By all means, in everything, in all respects, may they be entirely successful in their journeys! After bowing with the head to the feet of the Omniscient One i.e., the Buddha, the mahānāvika Buddhagupta, resident of Raktamr̥ttika, formulates the above prayer.

The text is laid out in four zones, on the front and three sides of a stone slab whose front also bears the depiction of a stūpa. The line of text on the upper side was included in Low's and Laidlay's facsimiles but excluded in subsequently produced estampages and editions. It is here tentatively taken to be the first line intended to be read after the stanza. On the side to the viewer's right, the first line has also been ignored by previous scholars: from the traces of writing that remain visible on the estampages, we infer that it may have started with an expression like sattvarāśayaḥ for all beings, or sarvajñāna for omniscience — the latter being our preferred hypothesis, given the inscription's focus on knowledge (jñāna) —, and ended with a word like praṇidhi, praṇidhāna, or prārthanā.

The opening stanza is attested throughout maritime Southeast Asia in the second half of the first millennium CE, but has not yet been traced to any Buddhist scripture (). The relevance to the rest of the inscription of the version of the Supāragajātaka contained in Āryaśūra's Jātakamālā was first observed by Chhabra (194) and discussed by Nilakanta Sastri (). The story concerns a group of merchants from Bharukaccha (Broach), wishing to undertake a voyage to Suvarṇabhūmi, who consult an old navigator called Supāraga, residing in Śūrpāraka (Sopara).Thus the reading of the name of the port city in the most recent edition of the Sanskrit text (1126). The navigator is none other than a former incarnation of the Buddha, and the Bodhisattva's name is explained as follows: tasya parama-siddhayātratvāt supāraga ity eva nāma babhūva, he was called Supāraga (easily going to the opposite shore, also implying: fully conversant) because he was the acme of success is traveling. Given the old and widespread association of Suvarṇabhūmi with parts of Southeast Asia (), including peninsular Malaysia, it seems quite possible that this version of the Supāragajātaka inspired the author of the inscription in elaborating on siddhayātra— although the expression siddhayātra is not unique to the Supāragajātaka in Buddhist literature.

What does not seem to have been observed in previous scholarship is how, starting from sarveṇa prakāreṇa, the wording finds an extensive parallel in the explanation of the opening stanza in Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośabhāṣya: yaḥ sarvathā-sarva-hatāndhakāraḥ saṁsārapaṅkāj jagad ujjahāra | tasmai namaskr̥tya yathārthaśāstre śāstraṁ pravakṣyāmy abhidharmakośam || sarveṇa prakāreṇa sarvasmin hatāndhakāraḥ sarvathā-sarva-hatāndhakāraḥ | ajñānaṁ hi bhūtārtha-darśana-pratibandhādhakāram | tac ca bhagavato buddhasya pratipakṣa-lābhenātyantaṁ sarvathā sarvatra jñeye punar-anutpatti-dharmatvād dhatam | ato 'sau sarvathā-sarva-hatāndhakāraḥ |. While the opening stanza of the Abhidharmakośa, and the commentary on it, refer to the Buddha as sarvathā-sarva-hatāndhakāra, Universal Dispeller of Everyone's Darkness, in our inscription the place of a Buddha's epithet is taken by sarvathā-sarva-siddhayātra, designating all creatures aspiring to salvation, and possibly coined on the basis of the Supāragajātaka. In the Buddhist context of maritime Southeast Asia, the word siddhayātra is found in several inscriptions directly or indirectly associated with the kingdom of Sriwijaya, centered on Palembang in South Sumatra, Indonesia, sometimes in combination with sarvasattva (, ).

The term (mahā)nāvika implies a professional involvement in maritime navigation, and the absence of any monastic terminology suggests rather clearly that Buddhagupta was a layman (although has advised not to exclude that Buddhagupta was a monk); Raktamr̥ttika, meaning Red Earth, is famous as the name of a monastery near Karṇasuvarṇa, at a village now called Rangamati, also meaning Red Earth, not far south of modern Murshidabad in what is now West Bengal, India. Some scholars assume that this is where Buddhagupta originated (), but there are grounds for caution, not only because Buddhagupta the layman is unlikely to have been resident of a monastery (24-25) but also because toponyms having the same meaning are fairly common in South and Southeast Asia.Wheatley (33) observed that there are, for example, numerous instances of the typonym Tanah Merah on the present-day map of Malaya, and likewise there is more than one Rangamati in West Bengal and Bangladesh.

It remains uncertain whether we are on the mark with our restitution śrī-sarvvajña (only the syllable jña being legible on Laidlay's facsimile), but in this text focused on jñāna, the epithet sarvajña forms an ideally suitable designation of the Buddha, who is also symbolized as object of Buddhagupta's veneration by the stūpa around which the text is engraved.

Facsimiles by Low (, reprinted by several later scholars including Jacq-Hergoualc'h ) and Laidlay ). After a preliminary attempt by Rajendralal Mitra, reported by Laidlay (), the first serious attempt at decipherment was published by Kern (), and his results were refined by Chhabra (). Kern's publication was reprinted () and published in English version (); Chhabra's was reprinted too (). This digital edition based on the published facsimiles as well as and on estampages.

listBibl.bibl: Estampages, Leiden University Library, Kern Institute collection, no. N. 13
